one_line_takeaway: By iteratively identifying and jointly ablating ALL previously-found refusal directions during fine-tuning (not just the latest one), the authors force LLMs to encode refusal redundantly across causally-independent subspaces, cutting jailbreak ASR by 92-97% across four models while improving over-refusal compliance and preserving utility -- turning today's 'fail-open' refusal (single dominant direction, one suppression away from bypass) into 'fail-closed' alignment.
key_finding: Across all four models, the proposed method achieves the strongest overall jailbreak robustness: average ASR of 0.6-1.7% (e.g., Llama2-7B: 1.4±1.6%; Llama3-8B: 0.6±0.6%; Gemma2-2B: 1.7±1.0%; Gemma2-9B: 1.4±0.9%), reducing average ASR by 92.3-97.2% relative to the undefended model, versus 23.8-93.7% reduction achieved by baselines, and keeping ASR <=4% in every individual attack/model cell. It is particularly effective against the black-box PAIR attack (the only defense keeping PAIR's ASR below 17.5% on every model). It simultaneously achieves the best or second-best overrefusal compliance rate (CR) on every model, with the highest average CR among robust methods (86.4% vs 68.8-75.7% for baselines) and near-best utility accuracy (61.6% average vs 62.0% for the best baseline, CAT, a 0.8-point gap). The method transfers to LoRA fine-tuning (~5% of parameters), matching full fine-tuning ASR/Acc within ~0.6 points on Gemma2-9B and even improving CR (86.1% -> 94.4%). Mechanistic analysis on Llama3-8B (Figure 3) shows the ten learned directions are causally redundant under joint ablation: ASR rises from 0% to only 1% when ALL 10 training directions are removed simultaneously, versus 0% to 97% for a single-feature-ablation (SFA) variant that ablates only one direction per iteration -- demonstrating that the MFA (multi-feature, jointly-applied) operator, not merely training multiple directions, is what produces true redundancy. Under real jailbreak attacks, directions r1-r5 are suppressed by only 10-58% (vs. 43-71% suppression of the base model's single DIM direction), and different attacks suppress different subsets of directions (GCG: 10-15%; AutoDAN/PAIR: 53-58% on r1-r2 but only 25-36% on r3-r5), so no single direction is a uniform point of failure across attacks. Ablation study (Gemma2-2B) shows ASR decreases from 54.8% to 1.2% as iterations increase from 0 to 10, plateauing around iteration 7.
core_contribution: The paper identifies that current LLM refusal mechanisms are 'fail-open': although refusal behavior spans multiple latent features, it collapses onto a single dominant direction that prompt-based jailbreaks suppress to bypass alignment entirely (demonstrated via directional ablation with the DIM refusal direction, causing ASR to spike across four models). Building on this diagnosis, the authors propose 'fail-closed alignment' as a design principle -- refusal should be distributed across multiple independent, causally redundant pathways so no single direction is a point of failure -- and instantiate it with a concrete algorithm, 'Fail-Closed Alignment' (Algorithm 1): an iterative procedure that (1) identifies the model's current dominant refusal direction via gradient-based Refusal Direction Optimization (RDO, from Wollschläger et al. 2025), (2) builds a multi-feature ablation (MFA) operator via QR decomposition that projects out the span of ALL previously identified directions jointly (not just the latest one), and (3) fine-tunes the model to refuse harmful prompts under that ablation (safety loss) while regularizing toward the base model's output distribution on benign prompts via KL divergence (utility loss), forcing a new, independent refusal direction to emerge at each of K=10 iterations.
threat_model: White-box and black-box prompt-based jailbreak attackers against an aligned, instruction-tuned LLM. White-box: GCG (gradient-guided adversarial suffix search) and AutoDAN (evolutionary search using harmful-response likelihood as fitness). Black-box: PAIR (automated iterative red-teaming) and HumanJailbreaks (manually designed role-play/obfuscation templates from Shen et al. 2024). The paper also separately evaluates a stronger, representation-level 'auditing' threat model: an adversary with direct access to internal activations who ablates the DIM or RDO refusal directions outright (results reported in Appendix B.1, not in the main text extracted here).
limitations: Directions r6-r10 (the later-discovered directions in the 10-iteration run) show near-zero cosine similarity with activations on harmful prompts, i.e., they do not appear to activate the way earlier directions do; the authors speculate later iterations may push the model toward nonlinear refusal mechanisms not captured by linear feature identification, and explicitly leave this unexplained and label it future work. The method requires K=10 rounds of iterative fine-tuning (each round: direction identification via RDO + joint ablation + retraining), which the paper describes only as 'small computational overhead' without giving absolute wall-clock/GPU-hour numbers in the extracted main text (deferred to Appendix C). Evaluation is restricted to four prompt-based jailbreak families and does not cover other threat classes such as multi-turn attacks, fine-tuning-based jailbreaks, or many-shot jailbreaking. Llama2-7B required a different training procedure (SFT instead of the standard objective) due to poor baseline compliance, indicating the method's default recipe does not transfer uniformly across all base models. The paper only evaluates up to 9B-parameter models, so scaling behavior to larger models is untested in the paper.

datasets_used: CircuitBreaker adversarial training dataset (Zou et al. 2024) -- 5,000 harmful prompts, used as Dsafe
Alpaca (Taori et al. 2023) -- 4,500 prompts sampled for Dutil benign training set; also used for utility/overrefusal held-out eval (1000 prompts, disjoint from training)
XSTest (Röttger et al. 2024) -- 200 prompts in Dutil training set; 50 held-out prompts for overrefusal eval
HarmBench (Mazeika et al. 2024) and AdvBench (Zou et al. 2023) -- source of 200 sampled prompts for jailbreak ASR evaluation; HarmBench judge used to classify harmful responses
SaladBench (Li et al. 2024) -- dataset splits used within the RDO refusal-direction identification subroutine
BoolQ, RTE, HellaSwag, WinoGrande, ARC-C, OpenBookQA -- six zero-shot utility/accuracy benchmarks
quantitative_results: Average ASR reduction: 92.3-97.2% for the proposed method vs 23.8-93.7% for baselines, across four models
Llama2-7B: undefended average ASR 18.3±23.2% -> Ours 1.4±1.6% (No Attack 0.0, GCG 1.5, AutoDAN 0.0, PAIR 4.0, HumanJailbreaks 0.1)
Llama3-8B: undefended average ASR 9.8±8.3% -> Ours 0.6±0.6% (GCG 1.0, PAIR 1.5, all others 0.0)
Gemma2-2B: undefended average ASR 54.8±22.6% -> Ours 1.7±1.0% (GCG 3.0, AutoDAN 1.5, PAIR 2.0, HumanJailbreaks 0.2)
Gemma2-9B: undefended average ASR 50.0±18.7% -> Ours 1.4±0.9% (GCG 1.0, AutoDAN 0.5, PAIR 3.0, HumanJailbreaks 1.2)
Overrefusal compliance rate (CR): Ours achieves highest average 86.4% across models vs 68.8-75.7% average for baselines
Per-model CR (Ours): Llama2-7B 71.7%, Llama3-8B 95.2%, Gemma2-2B 92.6%, Gemma2-9B 86.1%
Utility accuracy: Ours 61.6% average vs best baseline CAT 62.0% (0.8-point gap); per-model Acc for Ours: Llama2-7B 57.9%, Llama3-8B 61.0%, Gemma2-2B 60.3%, Gemma2-9B 67.1%
LoRA (Gemma2-9B, rank 128, ~5% params): ASR 1.4% (full FT) vs 2.0% (LoRA); CR 86.1% (full FT) vs 94.4% (LoRA); Acc 67.1% (full FT) vs 67.2% (LoRA) -- within ~0.6 points of full fine-tuning
Mechanistic redundancy test (Llama3-8B, Figure 3): ablating all 10 learned directions jointly raises ASR from 0% to only 1% (Ours/MFA) vs 0% to 97% for single-feature-ablation (SFA) baseline variant

Direction suppression under jailbreaks (Llama3-8B): base model's single DIM direction suppressed 43-71% by attacks; the method's directions r1-r5 suppressed only 10-58% (GCG: 10-15%; AutoDAN/PAIR: 53-58% on r1-r2, 25-36% on r3-r5)

Ablation over jailbreak types in the motivating study (Figure 1): all four attacks reduce DIM refusal-direction activation by up to 75%, and ablating the DIM direction alone spikes ASR across all four base models
Training-iteration sensitivity (Gemma2-2B, Figure 4): ASR decreases 54.8% -> 1.2% as iterations K go from 0 to 10, plateauing around iteration 7, while CR and Acc remain stable
Utility-weight sensitivity (Gemma2-2B, Figure 4): increasing lambda from low to high improves CR from 40.8% to 94.5%
baselines_compared: Augmented refusal training (RT) (Qi et al. 2025) -- fine-tunes on safety-recovery examples (harmful prefix followed by refusal)
CAT (Xhonneux et al. 2024) -- crafts adversarial examples in embedding space for faster adversarial training
LAT (Chao et al. 2024, as cited by this paper for the latent-adversarial-training baseline) -- adversarially perturbs latent activations then fine-tunes to resist them

DeepRefusal (Xie et al. 2025) -- ablates only the single, statically computed DIM refusal direction across random layers/positions, then trains to continue refusing (closest prior work; the paper's method generalizes this to jointly ablating ALL previously identified directions)
No-defense / undefended base model (No Attack row) as the reference point for ASR/CR/Acc deltas
